speaker: interviewer
text: Please continue about-- we were talking about the number of relatives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Aunts, uncles, and cousins that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, between uncles, cousins on my mother's side, my father was a one and only child, but they had-- my-- my father had a lot of uncles, aunts with their children. As a matter of fact, one-- two of them survived the war together with me. And I wouldn't say [? alive, ?] I would say between 60 to 80 people of our families that perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message or thought would you like to leave for your grandson and for future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] First of all, I love you all. I love mankind. Everybody, you will always find something good in everybody. We each have something good to contribute. I hope, Marc, you will grow up to be a decent human being. That's what I said-- said to you when you were two and a half weeks old when I held you in my arms. And [SIGHS] I said that to my children, too. Always gave them speeches, be decent persons. And I think they are. Be decent. Be good. Treat everybody the way you would like to treat-- you to be treated yourself. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [SIGHS] My message to, I guess, human beings can be good people. Give them a chance. But don't ever let people bully you. Don't ever allow anyone to bully anybody. And don't be a part of any of that, one against the other because that's the worst that you could do to anybody. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm not saying give your life for somebody else. But see to it that nobody gets hurt.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much for sharing your story with us today.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. You're very welcome. This is the number that I was given in Auschwitz sometimes-- a few days after arrival. The number is A-19165. [BACKGROUND NOISE] This is my father, Baruch Wiener. The picture was taken in 1915.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in Cracow, Poland. This is my mother, Leah Wiener. That picture was taken in Cracow in 1915. This is my husband's father, Eli Horowitz. His-- this picture must have been taken about 1913, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Cracow, do you think or--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband's mother. Her name was Tauba Horowitz. That picture was taken in about 1914.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nowy Sacz. This is our wedding photograph. This was taken in Paris in July 1948.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my husband taken in 1990 in Tamarac. He wasn't feeling well anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my daughter, Leah Horowitz. That picture was taken in Washington, DC.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son, Eli, and his wife, Paula, their wedding picture. The picture was taken on September 8, 1984.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my grandson, Marc Horowitz. That picture was taken the-- in 1996, in January of '66-- 1996. What did I say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not '96. We're in '96 now. How-- how old?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry, yes. It was this recent picture. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: That is a spoon, a soup spoon given to me by a Russian woman in Auschwitz when she was being transported to a different-- on a different transport. And she told me that with that, I'll survive, because I'll have with what to eat. These are eyeglasses in which I survived Auschwitz. I held them in the palm of my hand when I was passing by Mengele. One of his undercommanders recognized me and said to me, you want to live, you keep-- take them off. But I couldn't be without them. So I-- I think I can claim I'm the only survivor in Auschwitz-- from Auschwitz that has eyeglasses-- that had eyeglasses on.
